title: Healthy vs. Unhealthy Religious Engagement
titleAr: الانخراط الديني الصحي مقابل غير الصحي
learningObjective: After this exercise, the user will be able to identify criteria for healthy religious engagement and recognize patterns of unhealthy engagement.
learningObjectiveAr: بعد هذا التمرين، سيتمكن المستخدم من تحديد معايير الانخراط الديني الصحي.

content.scenario:
Allport & Ross (1967) identified two religious orientations:

**Intrinsic**: Religion as an end in itself — lived, internalized values
**Extrinsic**: Religion as a means to an end — social status, belonging, comfort

**Healthy engagement criteria:**
✓ Space for questions and doubt
✓ Balance with other life areas
✓ Respect for others' choices
✓ Growth-oriented, not fear-driven
✓ Professional help welcomed alongside spiritual support

**Unhealthy patterns:**
✗ No room for doubt → rigidity
✗ Religious activity replacing all other life areas → imbalance
✗ Condemning others' different paths → exclusivity
✗ Fear-based motivation only → anxiety

items.text: A student balances religious study with academics, friendships, and self-care. → Healthy engagement (balanced)
items.explanation: Healthy. Balance across life areas is a key indicator of healthy religious engagement.

items.text: A student drops all hobbies, avoids non-religious friends, and feels guilty about any non-religious activity. → Unhealthy pattern (imbalance, guilt-driven)
items.explanation: Concerning. Dropping all non-religious activities and pervasive guilt suggests imbalanced, fear-driven engagement.

feedback.correct: Well evaluated! Healthy engagement is balanced, growth-oriented, and makes room for human complexity. Faith should enhance life, not restrict it.
feedback.incorrect: Review: healthy engagement balances faith with other life areas; unhealthy patterns show rigidity, isolation, or guilt.

whatNotToDo: Don't use these criteria to judge others' religiosity level
Don't assume that high religiosity = unhealthy — it's about quality, not quantity
evidence: Allport, G.W., & Ross, J.M. (1967). Personal religious orientation and prejudice. JPSP, 5(4), 432-443.
safetyNote: ⚠️ Psychology-of-religion perspective. Not a theological ruling.
com_b_mechanism: Intrinsic vs extrinsic assessment builds reflective motivation for authentic engagement
